resting.definition: Being in a state of calm or not doing anything active.
resting.example: Her resting expression looked peaceful and content.

mood.definition: How you feel at a particular time.
mood.example: His mood improved after a good meal.

usual.definition: What happens most of the time; normal.
usual.example: She arrived at her usual time.

surprise.definition: To make someone feel unexpected wonder.
surprise.example: The test results may surprise you.

fro.definition: In 'to and fro': moving back and forward between two points.
fro.example: The conversation went to and fro between them.

relating.definition: Connecting and communicating with another person.
relating.example: Relating well with your child takes time and patience.

investment.definition: Time or effort you put in now to get good results later.
investment.example: Spending time with your baby is a long-term investment.

tendency.definition: A natural way of behaving or thinking that happens often.
tendency.example: He has a tendency to worry about small things.

temperament.definition: A person's natural character or personality type.
temperament.example: Every baby is born with a different temperament.

habitually.definition: In a way that is done regularly as a habit.
habitually.example: She habitually wakes up early every morning.

relaxed.definition: Feeling calm and free from worry or tension.
relaxed.example: The baby seemed relaxed in her mother's arms.

attuned.definition: Being aware of and sensitive to someone's feelings and needs.
attuned.example: An attuned parent notices when their child is upset.

anxious.definition: Feeling worried or nervous about something.
anxious.example: She felt anxious before the exam.

default.definition: The usual or normal state when nothing else changes it.
default.example: His default mood was cheerful and calm.

unsoothed.example: An unsoothed baby may cry for a long time.

unmet.definition: Not satisfied or taken care of.
unmet.example: The child had many unmet emotional needs.

habitual.definition: Done regularly or as a habit.
habitual.example: His habitual response was to stay quiet.

stress.definition: To give special importance to something; to emphasize.
stress.example: I want to stress how important this is.

spectrum.definition: The full range from one end to the other.
spectrum.example: Children feel the whole spectrum of emotions.

emotions.definition: Strong feelings such as happiness, sadness, anger, or fear.
emotions.example: It's healthy for children to express their emotions.

tears.definition: Drops of water that come from your eyes when you cry.
tears.example: The sad movie brought tears to her eyes.

therapy.definition: Treatment for mental or emotional problems by talking to a professional.
therapy.example: Therapy helped her deal with her anxiety.

powerful.definition: Having a strong effect on people.
powerful.example: Music can be a powerful way to change your mood.

soothe.definition: To make someone feel calm and less upset.
soothe.example: She sang softly to soothe the crying baby.

adequately.definition: In a way that is enough or good enough.
adequately.example: The children were not adequately fed.

available.definition: Ready and able to be there for someone.
available.example: A parent should be available when their child needs them.

observe.definition: To watch carefully and pay attention.
observe.example: She liked to observe how her baby explored new toys.

engage.definition: To take part in something or interact with someone.
engage.example: Try to engage with your child during playtime.

valuable.example: Every child should feel valuable and loved.

development.definition: The process of growing and changing over time.
